course_info.title: Human Biology
course_info.meeting_schedule: Monday, Wednesday, Friday - 3 hours lecture + 2 hours lab per week

class_schedule.topic: Introduction to Human Biology and the Scientific Method
learning_objectives: Describe the levels of biological organization from atoms to organisms
Apply the scientific method to biological questions
Understand the characteristics that define living organisms
readings: NIH Introduction to the Scientific Method

lecture_notes.introduction: Human biology is the study of the structure, function, and behavior of the human body. This course provides the biological foundation essential for health informatics professionals who must understand the clinical data they manage.
key_concepts: Levels of organization: atoms, molecules, organelles, cells, tissues, organs, organ systems, organism
Characteristics of life: metabolism, homeostasis, growth, reproduction, response to stimuli
Scientific method: observation, hypothesis, experiment, analysis, conclusion
Experimental design: variables, controls, sample size, reproducibility
Evidence-based biology and its connection to evidence-based medicine
lecture_notes.demonstration: Interactive exercise identifying biological organization levels using the cardiovascular system as an example

review.value: The maintenance of a stable internal environment despite changes in external conditions - the central organizing principle of human physiology
Systematic approach to understanding nature through observation, hypothesis formation, experimentation, and evidence-based conclusion

learning_objectives: Identify the major organelles of a human cell and their functions
Explain how cell membranes regulate transport of materials
Understand the role of DNA in cell function and reproduction

lecture_notes.introduction: The cell is the fundamental unit of life. Understanding cell structure and function provides the foundation for understanding tissues, organs, and disease processes.
key_concepts: Cell membrane: phospholipid bilayer, transport proteins, selective permeability
Organelles: nucleus (DNA), mitochondria (energy), ribosomes (protein synthesis), ER, Golgi
Cell transport: passive (diffusion, osmosis), active (pumps, endocytosis, exocytosis)
DNA structure and replication: double helix, base pairing, semiconservative replication
Cell division: mitosis for growth and repair, meiosis for gamete production
lecture_notes.demonstration: Virtual microscopy lab examining human cell types: epithelial, muscle, nerve, blood cells

review.value: Organelle producing ATP through cellular respiration - the energy currency powering all cellular functions
Process of copying DNA before cell division: helicase unwinds, primase adds primers, DNA polymerase synthesizes new strands

learning_objectives: Describe the structure and function of the heart
Explain blood flow through the pulmonary and systemic circuits
Understand blood pressure regulation and common cardiovascular diseases

lecture_notes.introduction: Cardiovascular disease is the leading cause of death worldwide. Understanding the cardiovascular system is essential for interpreting the clinical data that dominates health informatics.
key_concepts: Heart anatomy: four chambers, valves, coronary arteries, conduction system
Cardiac cycle: systole (contraction) and diastole (relaxation)
Blood flow: right atrium, right ventricle, pulmonary arteries, lungs, pulmonary veins, left atrium, left ventricle, aorta
Blood pressure: systolic/diastolic, regulation by baroreceptors and RAAS
Cardiovascular diseases: atherosclerosis, hypertension, MI, heart failure, arrhythmias
lecture_notes.demonstration: Heart dissection (virtual or physical model) identifying all chambers, valves, and major vessels
setup_requirements: Heart model or virtual dissection software
Blood pressure cuffs for measurement practice

review.value: SA node (pacemaker) initiates impulse, AV node delays it, Bundle of His and Purkinje fibers distribute it - producing the ECG waveform
Buildup of plaque (cholesterol, fat, calcium) in arterial walls, narrowing the lumen and reducing blood flow - underlying cause of most heart attacks and strokes

learning_objectives: Explain the mechanics of breathing and gas exchange
Describe the organs and functions of the digestive system
Understand common diseases of the respiratory and digestive systems

lecture_notes.introduction: The respiratory and digestive systems provide the body with oxygen and nutrients while removing waste. Their diseases are among the most commonly coded diagnoses in health records.
key_concepts: Respiratory anatomy: nasal cavity, pharynx, larynx, trachea, bronchi, alveoli
Gas exchange: diffusion of O2 and CO2 across the respiratory membrane
Respiratory diseases: asthma, COPD, pneumonia, lung cancer, COVID-19
Digestive tract: mouth, esophagus, stomach, small intestine, large intestine
Digestive diseases: GERD, peptic ulcer, Crohn's, celiac, colorectal cancer
lecture_notes.demonstration: Lung capacity measurement lab using spirometry, connecting to clinical pulmonary function testing

review.value: O2 diffuses from alveolar air (high concentration) into blood (low concentration); CO2 diffuses opposite direction - driven by partial pressure gradients
Chronic Obstructive Pulmonary Disease: progressive airflow limitation from chronic bronchitis and/or emphysema, primarily caused by smoking

class_schedule.topic: The Immune System and Infectious Disease
learning_objectives: Describe the components of innate and adaptive immunity
Explain how vaccines work and the concept of herd immunity
Understand the biology of infectious disease relevant to public health informatics

lecture_notes.introduction: The immune system protects against infection. Understanding immunity is essential for informatics professionals working with immunization registries, infection surveillance, and outbreak management.
key_concepts: Innate immunity: skin barriers, phagocytes, complement, inflammation, fever
Adaptive immunity: B cells (antibodies), T cells (cell-mediated), memory cells
Humoral vs. cell-mediated immune responses
Vaccination: active immunization, herd immunity thresholds, vaccine types (mRNA, viral vector, inactivated)
Infectious disease biology: bacteria, viruses, fungi, parasites, prions
lecture_notes.demonstration: Simulated outbreak exercise: track the spread of an infection through a population and model the impact of different vaccination rates on herd immunity

review.value: When a sufficient proportion of a population is immune (through vaccination or prior infection), indirect protection is provided to non-immune individuals
Deliver mRNA instructions for cells to produce a viral protein (e.g., SARS-CoV-2 spike), triggering an immune response without using live virus

learning_objectives: Explain Mendelian genetics and common inheritance patterns
Understand the Human Genome Project and its implications
Describe how genomics is transforming personalized medicine

lecture_notes.introduction: Genomics is reshaping healthcare from one-size-fits-all to precision medicine. Health informatics professionals must understand genetic data types and their clinical applications.
key_concepts: Mendelian genetics: dominant, recessive, codominant, sex-linked inheritance
Human genome: 3 billion base pairs, ~20,000 protein-coding genes
Genetic testing: carrier screening, diagnostic testing, pharmacogenomics, direct-to-consumer
Precision medicine: using genetic information to guide treatment selection and dosing
Genomic data in EHRs: integration challenges, clinical decision support for pharmacogenomics
lecture_notes.demonstration: Pedigree analysis exercise: determine inheritance patterns for genetic conditions from family history data, connecting to clinical genetics concepts

review.value: Using genetic information to predict drug response - e.g., CYP2D6 variants affecting codeine metabolism, guiding safer prescribing
Treatment approach tailoring medical care to individual characteristics including genetics, environment, and lifestyle

questions.question: Explain how the structure of the cell membrane enables selective permeability. Why is this property essential for maintaining homeostasis?
questions.answer: The cell membrane is a phospholipid bilayer with hydrophilic heads facing outward and hydrophobic tails facing inward, creating a barrier to water-soluble molecules. Embedded transport proteins (channels and pumps) allow specific molecules to cross. This selective permeability is essential for homeostasis because it allows cells to maintain different concentrations of ions (Na+, K+, Ca2+) inside vs. outside the cell, control water balance through osmosis, import nutrients while excluding toxins, and maintain the electrochemical gradient needed for nerve impulse transmission and muscle contraction.

questions.question: Trace the path of blood through the heart and lungs, starting from the superior vena cava. Identify what happens to the blood at each major stop.
questions.answer: Deoxygenated blood enters the right atrium via the superior and inferior vena cava. It passes through the tricuspid valve into the right ventricle. The right ventricle pumps it through the pulmonary valve into the pulmonary arteries, which carry it to the lungs. In the alveolar capillaries, CO2 is released and O2 is absorbed (gas exchange). Oxygenated blood returns via the pulmonary veins to the left atrium. It passes through the mitral (bicuspid) valve into the left ventricle. The left ventricle (the strongest chamber) pumps it through the aortic valve into the aorta, which distributes oxygenated blood to the entire body.

rubric.key: Correct pathway with all chambers and valves named
Gas exchange in lungs correctly described
Distinction between pulmonary and systemic circuits

questions.question: Compare innate and adaptive immunity. How do they work together to protect against a bacterial infection?
questions.answer: Innate immunity provides immediate, non-specific defense: skin and mucous membranes block entry, phagocytes (neutrophils, macrophages) engulf bacteria, complement proteins lyse bacteria, and inflammation recruits more immune cells. Adaptive immunity is slower but specific: B cells produce antibodies targeting bacterial antigens, helper T cells coordinate the response, and memory cells provide long-lasting protection. Working together: innate immunity responds first (minutes to hours) containing the infection while adaptive immunity develops (days). Macrophages present bacterial antigens to T cells, activating the adaptive response. Antibodies then enhance innate mechanisms by opsonizing bacteria for more efficient phagocytosis. Memory cells provide faster response upon re-exposure.

rubric.key: Accurate description of innate immunity components
Accurate description of adaptive immunity components
Clear explanation of how they work together

questions.question: A genetic test reveals that a patient has a CYP2D6 poor metabolizer genotype. Explain what this means clinically and why this information is relevant to health informatics.
questions.answer: CYP2D6 is a liver enzyme that metabolizes approximately 25% of all drugs. A poor metabolizer has two non-functional alleles, meaning they metabolize CYP2D6 substrates very slowly. Clinical impact: drugs metabolized by CYP2D6 (e.g., codeine, tramadol, tamoxifen, many antidepressants) may accumulate to toxic levels at standard doses, or prodrugs (like codeine, which requires CYP2D6 to convert to morphine) may be ineffective. Health informatics relevance: (1) This genetic result should be stored in the EHR as a discrete data element. (2) CDS should trigger pharmacogenomic alerts when CYP2D6 substrates are ordered for this patient. (3) The result needs to be coded using standardized vocabulary (e.g., LOINC for the test, pharmacogenomic annotation) for interoperability. (4) This represents the kind of precision medicine data that EHRs must increasingly support.

rubric.key: Correct explanation of CYP2D6 poor metabolizer

major_assignments.title: Human Biology Systems Integration Report
major_assignments.description: Select a common chronic disease (diabetes, heart failure, COPD, or chronic kidney disease) and write a comprehensive report explaining the biological mechanisms of the disease across all relevant body systems, connecting the biology to clinical data elements captured in health information systems.
objectives: Demonstrate understanding of human biology across multiple organ systems
Explain disease pathophysiology at the cellular and organ system level
Connect biological concepts to clinical data elements in health records
Synthesize information from multiple sources into a coherent analysis
deliverables: Written report (10-12 pages) covering pathophysiology, affected systems, diagnostic markers, and informatics connections
Diagram showing disease mechanism across organ systems
Table mapping biological concepts to clinical data elements (lab values, vital signs, ICD codes)

criteria: Clinical data elements correctly mapped to biology
Lab values and their biological significance explained
ICD-10 codes and clinical terminology used accurately
Understanding of how biology drives data in health records
Logical flow from cellular to systemic
Scientifically accurate disease pathophysiology across systems
Clear connection between biology and clinical informatics data

weekly_quizzes.description: Regular assessment of biological concepts and body systems

laboratory.description: Hands-on lab exercises including microscopy, dissection, and physiology experiments

major_assignments.description: Systems integration report connecting biology to health informatics

midterm_examination.description: Comprehensive assessment of cell biology, cardiovascular, and respiratory systems

final_examination.description: Cumulative exam covering all body systems including immune system and genetics

course_policies.attendance: Lab sessions are mandatory. Missing more than 2 labs without documented excuse results in grade reduction. Lecture attendance strongly encouraged.
course_policies.late_submissions: Late work loses 10% per day. Lab reports due within one week of the lab session.
course_policies.collaboration: Lab work is done in pairs. All written assignments and exams are individual work.
course_policies.academic_integrity: All work must be original. Lab reports must be individually written even when data is collected in pairs.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Lab coat and safety goggles for in-person labs. Virtual microscopy software access provided. Internet access for research assignments.
